7.note: Stephen cites Israel's explosive growth as the providential prelude to oppression: 'the people grew and multiplied in Egypt.' The Abrahamic blessing (Gen 17:2) reaches its fullest OT expression precisely when it triggers the enslavement that God will answer with exodus — the pattern of blessing-through-suffering that reaches its apex in the cross.

8.note: The new king 'who did not know Joseph' is Stephen's hinge point in his sermon: the transition from favor to slavery marks the moment Israel's groaning begins, which God will answer through Moses and, ultimately, through Christ. Ignorance of the covenant mediator leads to the oppression that demands a deliverer.

15.note: Pharaoh's decree to kill all newborn Hebrew boys is the OT type of Herod's massacre of the innocents: a pagan ruler attempts to eliminate the divinely appointed deliverer at birth. The pattern — earthly power targeting the promised Seed — recurs because Revelation 12:4 names the dragon as its author throughout redemptive history.

22.note: Pharaoh's final edict ('every son that is born you shall cast into the Nile') forces Moses' family to place him in an ark on the river — his miraculous preservation setting up the type Matthew fulfills when the infant Jesus flees to Egypt and returns, with Hosea 11:1 ('out of Egypt I called my son') as the explicit warrant.

1.note: Moses' parents hide him by faith

2.note: The Hall of Faith credits Moses' parents: 'by faith Moses was hidden for three months, because they saw he was beautiful, and they were not afraid of the king's edict.' Their defiance of Pharaoh's death-decree mirrors the Father's protection of the Son from Herod's sword — both preservations secure the deliverer appointed before time.

Stephen says Moses was 'beautiful in God's sight' (asteios tō theō) — divine delight in the infant deliverer. The Father's pleasure in Moses anticipates the voice at Jesus' baptism: 'This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased' (Matt 3:17), marking both deliverers for their missions.

10.note: Moses drawn from the water (the meaning of his name) prefigures what Paul calls being 'baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea.' The ark-basket is the instrument of preservation; baptism is the sacrament of passing through judgment into new life. Both Moses and the baptized Christian are 'drawn out' to serve the one who sent them.

11.note: Moses' voluntary identification with the suffering Hebrews rather than with Pharaoh's household is the great faith-act the author of Hebrews calls 'choosing to be mistreated with the people of God rather than enjoy the fleeting pleasures of sin, considering disgrace for the sake of Christ greater riches than the treasures of Egypt.' The shape of Moses' downward path prefigures the incarnation.
Moses chooses suffering with God's people

14.note: The Israelite's rebuke — 'Who made you a ruler and judge over us?' — becomes Stephen's interpretive key: at his first coming, Moses was rejected by his own people (v.35: 'this Moses, whom they rejected, God sent as both ruler and redeemer'). Stephen deploys the Moses typology to show that Israel repeated the pattern with Jesus — rejecting the God-sent deliverer twice.

24.note: God hears Israel's groaning and 'remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob.' Mary's Magnificat celebrates the same covenant memory: 'he has helped his servant Israel, in remembrance of his mercy, as he spoke to our fathers, to Abraham and to his offspring forever.' The incarnation is the ultimate fulfillment of what the exodus only began.

25.note: God 'saw the people of Israel — God knew.' This divine knowing (Heb. yāda') precedes the exodus commission. Stephen quotes God's parallel words at the burning bush: 'I have surely seen the mistreatment of my people...I have come down to deliver them.' Divine seeing always precedes divine sending, whether Moses to Egypt or the Son into the world (John 3:17).

2.note: Stephen cites the burning bush directly: 'an angel appeared to him in the wilderness of Sinai in a flame of fire in a bush.' Early church interpreters identified the Angel of the LORD as the pre-incarnate Christ — the same one who would later be baptized with fire (Matt 3:11) and of whom the Father says 'he makes his angels flames of fire' (Heb 1:7).

The burning bush that is not consumed embodies the paradox of divine holiness: fire that does not destroy. Hebrews 12:29, citing Deuteronomy 4:24, applies 'our God is a consuming fire' to the New Covenant assembly — more privileged than Moses, yet standing before the same holy God whose fire can refine or consume depending on one's response.

5.note: Stephen quotes verbatim: 'Take off your sandals from your feet, for the place where you are standing is holy ground.' The ground becomes holy by the presence of the holy God — anticipating John the Baptist's confession that he is unworthy even to untie the sandal of the one who sanctifies all ground he stands on (John 1:27).

6.note: Jesus cites this verse to establish bodily resurrection: 'I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob — he is not God of the dead, but of the living.' The divine self-identification with the patriarchs by name implies their continued personal existence and anticipated resurrection. The God of the burning bush is the God of Easter.

Mark records Jesus asking: 'have you not read in the book of Moses, in the passage about the bush, how God spoke to him: I am the God of Abraham, and the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob?' The burning bush pericope is named as the locus of NT resurrection theology — the eternal present-tense 'I AM' makes the patriarchs still living.

Luke: 'even Moses showed, in the passage about the bush, that the dead are raised.' The God who revealed himself in fire to Moses is the God who raises the dead — the same continuity Stephen exploits in Acts 7 to show Jesus as the culmination of the bush-God's purposes.

Stephen recounts that Moses 'trembled and did not dare to look' at the divine presence. The awe appropriate to the burning bush is the awe owed to the Son who is 'the radiance of the glory of God' (Heb 1:3) — the one before whom every knee will bow (Phil 2:10).

I am the God of Abraham the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob — Jesus cites Exod 3:6 to prove the resurrection: if YHWH called himself the God of the patriarchs in the present tense after their deaths, they must be alive; the burning bush is the resurrection's OT proof-text
I am the God of your fathers -- Stephen's speech
I am the God of Abraham -- God of the living not the dead

7.note: 'I have surely seen the affliction of my people...and I have come down to deliver them.' Zechariah's Benedictus opens: 'Blessed be the Lord God of Israel, for he has visited and redeemed his people.' Luke uses the exodus logic — God sees, God comes down, God redeems — as the template for the incarnation. Jesus is the fuller 'coming down.'

The burning bush commission — 'bring my people out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery' — is the archetypal liberation mandate. Jesus' reading of Isaiah 61 in the Nazareth synagogue ('to proclaim release to the captives...to set at liberty those who are oppressed') declares himself the greater Moses sent to accomplish what the exodus only prefigured.

10.note: God tells Moses: 'Come, I will send you to Pharaoh.' Stephen quotes this commission as the parallel to God's sending of Christ: the God who saw affliction sent his servant. The pattern — divine compassion → divine sending — structures both testaments; the Father who sent Moses ultimately 'sent his Son' (Gal 4:4).

12.note: 'I will be with you' — the pledge of divine presence with the commissioned deliverer. Jesus closes the Great Commission with the same guarantee: 'I am with you always, to the end of the age.' The Mosaic promise was for one generation's mission; the risen Christ's promise covers every generation until the consummation.

God's pledge 'I will be with you' to Moses at the bush is the OT form of Immanuel: 'God with us.' Matthew brackets his gospel with this name (1:23) and this promise (28:20), showing that the entire Christ-story is the narrative of God fulfilling what the burning bush only announced.

14.note: YHWH's self-disclosure — 'I AM WHO I AM' (Heb. 'ehyeh 'asher 'ehyeh) — is the Name Jesus claims in John 8:58: 'before Abraham was, I AM.' The ego eimi formula echoes throughout the Fourth Gospel (bread, light, gate, shepherd, resurrection, way, vine — 6:35; 8:12; 10:9; 11:25; 14:6; 15:1). Jesus does not merely bear the Name; he is the one who spoke from the burning bush.

'I AM WHO I AM' — being without origin or limit, past, present, and future. Revelation renders this: 'I am the Alpha and the Omega...who is and who was and who is to come, the Almighty' (1:8). The threefold tense unpacks the eternal Name Moses received at Horeb, now spoken by the risen Christ.

I AM WHO I AM — the divine name ehyeh asher ehyeh revealed at the burning bush; Jesus's seven I AM statements (John) and especially John 8:58 ('before Abraham was, I am') are deliberate invocations of the Exodus 3:14 divine name, claiming for Jesus the YHWH-identity

15.note: 'This is my name forever, my memorial from generation to generation.' The unchanging, eternal divine name corresponds to the NT confession: 'Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, today, and forever.' What the Name declared of the LORD — permanent, memorial, generational — is now confessed of the Son who fully reveals the Father.

God gives Moses a name that is a memorial through all generations — then in the fullness of time grants Jesus 'the name that is above every name, so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow.' The divine Name entrusted to Moses at the bush is the Name exalted universally in the resurrection and ascension of Christ.

20.note: God pledges: 'I will stretch out my hand and strike Egypt with all my wonders.' Stephen catalogs the fulfillment: Moses 'performed wonders and signs in Egypt and at the Red Sea.' The signs of the exodus are the template for the signs Jesus performs (John 20:30) — both authenticate the divine commission of the deliverer.

10.note: Alas, Sovereign LORD, I do not know how to speak; I am too young. But the LORD said to me... I will put my words in your mouth — Jeremiah's call-narrative repeats the identical pattern of Exodus 4:10-12: prophet resists the commission citing speech inability; YHWH rebukes and promises to give the words. The Moses-Jeremiah parallel shows that prophetic reluctance is a canonical pattern, not a character flaw, and that the prophetic word originates in divine provision, not human eloquence.

22.note: And so was fulfilled what the Lord had said through the prophet: Out of Egypt I called my son — Matthew quotes Hos 11:1 (out of Egypt I called my son) as fulfilled in the holy family's return from Egypt. Hosea's reference to Israel is itself grounded in Exod 4:22-23, where YHWH first declares Israel his firstborn son and commands his release from Egypt. The Israel-as-son identification that originates in Exodus is applied to Jesus through Hosea: as Israel was YHWH's son called from Egypt, so Jesus is the Son called from Egypt in a new exodus.

For those God foreknew he also predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son, that he might be the firstborn among many brothers — Paul's description of Christ as the firstborn among many brothers depends on Exod 4:22's identification of Israel as YHWH's firstborn son. Christ as the ultimate firstborn Son recapitulates and fulfills the Israel-sonship that the Exodus narrative establishes; the many brothers are those conformed to the image of this firstborn.

2.note: Who is like the beast? Who can wage war against it? — the beast's exaltation in Revelation (who is like the beast?) echoes the structural defiance of Pharaoh in Exod 5:2 (who is the LORD, that I should obey him?). Both texts express the anti-confessional hubris of imperial power that refuses to acknowledge YHWH's sovereignty; both contexts end with the display of YHWH's decisive authority over the defiant power.

22.note: How long, LORD, must I call for help, but you do not listen? Or cry out to you, Violence! but you do not save? — Moses's complaint in Exod 5:22 (why have you brought trouble on this people? Why did you send me?) is the progenitor of the lament-complaint tradition canonized in Habakkuk. Both texts represent the covenant servant returning to YHWH in honest protest when the divine plan appears to produce more harm than help; both receive a divine response that reframes the immediate suffering in the larger redemptive purpose.

3.note: I have revealed your name to those you gave me out of the world — Jesus's claim in the high-priestly prayer to have revealed the divine Name picks up the progressive-revelation theme of Exod 6:3, where YHWH tells Moses that his name (the LORD/YHWH) had not been fully made known to the patriarchs. What was partially disclosed to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (as El Shaddai) and more fully at Sinai (as YHWH) is completely revealed by Christ: the final and definitive disclosure of the divine Name is accomplished in the Son.

6.note: Praise be to the Lord, the God of Israel, because he has come to his people and redeemed them. He has raised up a horn of salvation for us in the house of his servant David — Zechariah's Benedictus uses the vocabulary of Exod 6:6 (I will redeem you with an outstretched arm) and the Exodus-liberation pattern to interpret the coming of Christ. The NT Exodus begins in Luke 1 with the same redemption-language and outstretched-arm imagery that YHWH used to announce the Egyptian exodus.

7.note: And I heard a loud voice from the throne saying, Now the dwelling of God is with men, and he will live with them. They will be his people, and God himself will be with them and be their God — Revelation's eschatological fulfillment formula (he will be their God; they will be his people) is the direct quotation and climactic fulfillment of the covenant formula first spoken in Exod 6:7: I will take you as my own people and I will be your God. The six-word covenant formula that originates in the Egyptian exodus reaches its final realization in the new Jerusalem.

I will be their God and they will be my people — Jeremiah's new covenant formulation quotes Exod 6:7 exactly and applies it to the eschatological covenant that supersedes the broken Mosaic covenant. The covenant formula initiated in Egypt (I will be your God; you will be my people) persists through the Mosaic, Davidic, and new covenants until its final fulfillment in the new Jerusalem (Rev 21:3).

3.note: Therefore God has mercy on whom he wants to have mercy, and he hardens whom he wants to harden — Paul's statement about divine sovereignty directly generalizes the hardening-of-Pharaoh principle that runs through Exod 7:3. The repeated hardening formula (I will harden Pharaoh's heart) is not incidental; Paul reads it as the paradigm for understanding how YHWH can work through resistant human agents to accomplish his redemptive purpose.

17.note: When you have lifted up the Son of Man, then you will know that I am he — the Exodus recognition formula (by this you will know that I am the LORD) runs through all ten plagues as their theological core: each plague is designed to produce knowledge of YHWH. Jesus's I AM declaration at the cross applies the same formula Christologically: the lifting up of the Son of Man is the supreme plague-event that produces the knowledge of his identity.

20.note: The second angel poured out his bowl on the sea, and it turned into blood like that of a dead person... the third angel poured out his bowl on the rivers and springs of water, and they became blood — Revelation's bowl judgments on the waters systematically reprise the first plague of Exodus 7. The Nile-to-blood plague that demonstrated YHWH's power over Egypt's life-source becomes the eschatological pattern for the final judgments on the world's waters.

19.note: But if I drive out demons by the finger of God, then the kingdom of God has come upon you — Jesus's claim to act by the finger of God in exorcism deliberately echoes the magicians' grudging acknowledgment in Exod 8:19: this is the finger of God. Both contexts pit the power of YHWH/God's kingdom directly against counterfeit spiritual power (Egyptian magic / demonic power), and in both cases the finger-of-God power is decisively superior.

22.note: You are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's special possession — Peter's description of the church draws on the Deuteronomic election vocabulary grounded in the plague distinction: I will make a distinction between my people and your people (Exod 8:22-23). The divine act of setting apart Israel from Egypt in the plagues is the prototype of the election that constitutes the new covenant community as a distinct people who belong to God.

16.note: For Scripture says to Pharaoh: I raised you up for this very purpose, that I might display my power in you and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth — Paul quotes Exod 9:16 directly in his argument about divine sovereignty in election. The explicit divine purpose articulated here (YHWH raised Pharaoh up in order to display his power and spread his name through Pharaoh's resistance) becomes the textual foundation for Paul's argument that God's election operates through a logic of sovereign purpose, not human merit or desert.
I raised you up for this very purpose — Pharaoh

27.note: Not everyone who says to me, Lord, Lord, will enter the kingdom of heaven, but only the one who does the will of my Father — Pharaoh's repeated confession-under-pressure followed by reversal-when-relief-comes (9:27: the LORD is in the right; I have sinned — followed by renewed hardening) is the Exodus portrait of the confession that does not translate into sustained obedience. Jesus's warning in Matt 7:21 addresses the same pattern: verbal acknowledgment without conforming one's life to the LORD's will.

2.note: We will tell the next generation the praiseworthy deeds of the LORD... so the next generation would know them, even the children yet to be born, and they in turn would tell their children — the pedagogical purpose YHWH states in Exod 10:2 (so that you may tell your children and grandchildren how I dealt with the Egyptians) is the program that Psalm 78 carries out: the Exodus plagues are the content of the intergenerational narrative that preserves covenant identity and grounds ongoing trust in YHWH.

23.note: The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it — the ninth plague (total darkness over Egypt for three days, while the Israelites had light in their dwellings) is the Exodus archetype for John's prologue contrast. The world's darkness is Egyptian darkness — judgment, death, and the absence of divine presence — while the light persisting in Israel anticipates the true light that came into the world in Christ.

5.note: By faith he kept the Passover and the application of blood, so that the destroyer of the firstborn would not touch the firstborn of Israel — Hebrews reads the Passover-blood application as an act of faith: Moses and Israel trusted YHWH's word that blood on the doorframes would protect against the final plague. The destroyer of the firstborn (Exod 11:5; 12:29) is the agent that Hebrews holds at bay through the faith-response to divine instruction.

3.note: Passover lamb slain — Christ our Passover sacrificed for us

5.note: You were redeemed... with the precious blood of Christ, a lamb without blemish or defect — Peter's description of Christ's redemptive work uses the exact Passover-lamb specification of Exod 12:5 (year-old males without defect). The qualitative demand of the Passover sacrifice (physical perfection) corresponds to the moral-ontological demand met by Christ: the lamb that redeems must be without blemish, and the only truly unblemished life is Christ's.

13.note: Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed — Paul explicitly identifies the Passover sacrifice of Exod 12 with the crucifixion of Christ. The blood on the doorframes (v.13: when I see the blood, I will pass over you) is fulfilled in Christ's blood: the divine wrath that passed over Israel when it saw the blood now passes over all who shelter under Christ's atoning death.

The Passover lamb without blemish — John the Baptist's Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world (John 1:29) identifies Jesus as the Passover Lamb; the Gospel of John times the crucifixion to coincide with the Passover lamb slaughter (John 19:14)

21.note: Moses institutes the Passover by faith

22.note: A jar of wine vinegar was there, so they soaked a sponge in it, put it on a stalk of hyssop, and lifted it to Jesus's lips — John's inclusion of hyssop at the crucifixion is deliberately Passover-referential: in Exod 12:22, hyssop is the instrument used to apply the Passover blood to the doorframes. At the cross, hyssop again appears at the moment of the Passover-lamb's death; the instrument that applied the protective blood at the first Passover appears again at the definitive Passover sacrifice.

46.note: These things happened so that the Scripture would be fulfilled: not one of his bones will be broken — John explicitly cites Exod 12:46 (do not break any of the bones of the Passover lamb) as fulfilled in the soldiers' decision not to break Jesus's legs. This is the only plague/Passover verse that a NT author explicitly labels as fulfilled Scripture in the crucifixion narrative, making it the clearest canonical link between the Passover lamb and Christ.

2.note: Luke quotes Exodus 13:2 directly in the presentation narrative: 'every firstborn male is to be consecrated to the Lord.' Jesus is presented at the Temple in fulfillment of this law — but as the one who fulfills it by being the true firstborn, not merely by performing the rite for him. The law of firstborn-consecration reaches its intended referent in the one who is the firstborn of all creation (Col 1:15).

Consecrate to me every firstborn — the firstborn-consecration institution in Exodus 13 is the OT type for the Pauline Christological title: Jesus is 'the firstborn of all creation,' 'the firstborn from among the dead.' The type points beyond every human firstborn to the one who is the ultimate Firstborn whom God claimed from among the dead.

9.note: This shall be a sign on your hand and a reminder between your eyes — the Deuteronomic and Exodus commands to bind the law between the eyes and on the hand became the practice of wearing phylacteries (tefillin); Jesus critiques the Pharisees for broadening their phylacteries to make them conspicuous, showing that the concrete application of this verse was a live religious practice in the first century.

19.note: Moses took the bones of Joseph with him, for Joseph had made the Israelites swear an oath — Hebrews 11:22 names Joseph's deathbed instruction about his bones as the act of faith that defines his place in the roll of the faithful: 'by faith Joseph, when he was dying, mentioned the exodus of the Israelites and gave instructions about his burial.' Joseph's faith was expressed in the certainty that God would bring the promised exodus to pass.

21.note: The LORD went before them by day in a pillar of cloud and by night in a pillar of fire — Paul explicitly calls these events typoi (1 Cor 10:6,11): 'our fathers were all under the cloud and all passed through the sea, and all were baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea.' The pillar of cloud and the sea-crossing together constitute the type for Christian baptism. The cloud-covering, like the sea-immersion, signifies the union with the mediator that baptism enacts.

The LORD went before them to guide their way — the divine leadership through the wilderness is the OT prototype for the Lamb's shepherding of the redeemed in Revelation: 'the Lamb at the center of the throne will be their shepherd; he will lead them to springs of living water.' The pillar that led Israel through the wilderness is fulfilled in the Lamb who leads the redeemed to their rest.
Israel under the cloud and through the sea

13.note: Do not be afraid. Stand firm and see what deliverance the LORD will bring today — Moses's command on the shore of the sea is the OT seed for the NT confidence that God himself fights on behalf of his people. Paul's 'if God is for us, who can be against us?' (Rom 8:31) draws on the same divine-warrior conviction that Moses expressed: when YHWH fights, the enemy is already defeated before the battle is joined.

14.note: The LORD himself will fight for you; you need only be still — the divine-warrior assurance that God battles on behalf of those who trust him provides the OT ground for Hebrews' confidence citation: 'the Lord is my helper; I will not be afraid. What can human beings do to me?' The posture of stillness while God fights is the inverse of anxious self-defense.

21.note: The LORD drove the sea back with a powerful east wind; the Israelites walked through on dry ground — Paul's typological reading identifies the sea-crossing explicitly as a baptism type: 'all were baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea.' The sea divides; Israel passes through; the waters close over their enemies. The structure maps onto baptism: passage through water as death-and-new-life, with union with the mediator as its meaning.

22.note: The Israelites walked through the sea on dry ground — Paul interprets the Red Sea crossing as a baptismal type: all were baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea; the exodus deliverance through water prefigures Christian baptism

By faith the people crossed the Red Sea as on dry land — the author of Hebrews includes the Red Sea crossing in the Hall of Faith; the Exodus generation's act of walking through the sea was faith in YHWH's promise

29.note: The Israelites walked through the sea on dry ground, with walls of water on their right and on their left — Hebrews 11:29 identifies the crossing as an act of faith: 'by faith the people passed through the Red Sea as on dry land; but when the Egyptians tried to do so, they were drowned.' The same act that was salvation for Israel (faith) was destruction for Egypt (presumption). Faith and the absence of faith determine the outcome of the same event.

31.note: The people feared the LORD and trusted in the LORD and in his servant Moses — the pattern of believing in the LORD and in his servant through witnessing signs of power recurs in John's Gospel: 'Jesus performed this first sign at Cana... and his disciples put their faith in him.' The Red Sea liberation is the foundational pattern for how divine sign-works generate faith in both the God who acts and the servant through whom he acts.

1.note: Moses and the Israelites sang this song to the LORD — Revelation 15:3 names the eschatological hymn of the redeemed 'the song of God's servant Moses and of the Lamb,' directly linking it to the Song of the Sea. John's redeemed community, standing on the glassy sea, sings the Mosaic song in its ultimate fulfillment: the final exodus from all that oppresses, the definitive drowning of the enemy.

2.note: The LORD is my strength and my song; he has become my salvation — this verse was preserved verbatim in Psalm 118:14 and Isaiah 12:2, indicating it became a standard formula of salvation-praise in Israel's liturgical tradition. Its use in all three contexts (Exod 15, Ps 118, Isa 12) shows how the Red Sea deliverance functioned as the paradigm of divine rescue that later generations applied to their own situations.

11.note: Who is like you among the gods, O LORD? — the incomparability question of Exodus 15 becomes the basis for Revelation 15:4: 'who will not fear you, Lord, and bring glory to your name? For you alone are holy.' Revelation's eschatological praise draws explicitly on the Song of the Sea; where Exodus 15 posed the rhetorical question of divine incomparability after defeating Egypt, Revelation 15 announces its universal answer: all nations will come and worship.

13.note: In your steadfast love you have led the people you redeemed — the Song of the Sea's declaration of redemptive leadership provides the OT framework for the Lamb's redemption-song in Revelation 5: 'you were slain, and with your blood you purchased for God persons from every tribe and language and people and nation.' The Red Sea redemption that Exodus 15 celebrates is the type for the blood-purchased redemption that Revelation celebrates.

17.note: You will bring them in and plant them on your own mountain — the holy mountain where God will dwell with his people is the destination of the exodus, here already envisioned in the Song of the Sea. Hebrews 12:22 announces this destination as already arrived: 'you have come to Mount Zion, to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem.' The mountain-planting of Exodus 15 reaches its eschatological fulfillment in the new covenant community's access to the heavenly Zion.

The sanctuary that your hands have established — the promised divine-dwelling on the holy mountain anticipates the new Jerusalem's defining characteristic: 'God's dwelling place is now among the people, and he will dwell with them.' The Song of the Sea's vision of a permanent sanctuary for YHWH among his redeemed people runs forward through the OT temple theology to its fulfillment in John's vision of the city where 'the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are its temple.'

18.note: The LORD will reign forever and ever — the closing declaration of the Song of the Sea finds its direct NT fulfillment in the seventh trumpet of Revelation: 'the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Messiah, and he will reign for ever and ever.' Exodus 15:18 is the OT anticipation; Revelation 11:15 is its eschatological realization. The eternal kingship celebrated after the Red Sea crossing reaches its final form when the last enemy is overcome.

20.note: Miriam the prophetess took a tambourine and all the women followed her with tambourines and dancing — the festal dancing after the great deliverance is the OT background for the feasting and music at the father's welcome of the prodigal (Luke 15:25); the joy that erupts at recovery from loss belongs to the same pattern of the community's exultation over divine rescue.

26.note: I am the LORD who heals you (YHWH Rapha) — the divine self-identification as healer is the OT ground for the NT promise of healing prayer: 'the prayer offered in faith will make the sick person well; the Lord will raise them up.' James's promise of healing rests on the character of God as the one who heals, first declared in the wilderness of Shur. The covenant title YHWH Rapha establishes healing as an expression of the divine covenant-character.

The leaves of the tree of life are for the healing of the nations (Rev 22:2) — the healing that YHWH promised in the wilderness covenant (Exod 15:26: no disease if you obey) reaches its eschatological form in the new creation, where the tree of life provides healing that the wilderness setting could only anticipate. The Exodus covenant-healing points forward to the universal, permanent healing of the new Jerusalem.

4.note: Jesus' interlocutors cite Psalm 78:24 ('he gave them bread from heaven to eat') which itself echoes this verse. In John 6 the manna is the type: Jesus declares himself 'the bread that comes down from heaven' — not merely supplying bread but being the bread. The daily provision to Israel was a shadow of the permanent provision in Christ.

Paul calls the manna 'spiritual food' — pointing back to Exodus 16:4 as supernatural provision from God, not merely natural food. The wilderness generation 'ate the same spiritual food' as the church eats spiritual food in the Eucharist; both are participations in the same divine provision, though one is shadow and one is substance.

Behold I am about to rain bread from heaven for you — the manna in the wilderness is the backdrop for Jesus's Bread of Life discourse; the crowd cites Ps 78:24 (he gave them bread from heaven) and Jesus corrects: my Father gives you the true bread from heaven, and I am that bread of life

15.note: 'What is it?' (Heb. man hû') becomes the name of the bread — Israel received something they could not classify or explain. Jesus' 'I am the bread of life' answers the ancient question: the one who came down from heaven is the true manna, the truly inexhaustible provision that the wilderness bread could only prefigure.

18.note: Paul quotes this verse directly to ground the Jerusalem collection in the manna miracle: 'whoever gathered much did not have too much, and whoever gathered little did not have too little.' The supernatural equality in the wilderness becomes the theological warrant for the church's economic solidarity — what God enforced by miracle, the Spirit cultivates by love.
Whoever gathered much did not have too much — manna

29.note: 'Remain each of you in his place; let no one go out on the seventh day' — the weekly rest from gathering manna is the anticipatory sabbath-pattern that Hebrews 4:9 declares still outstanding: 'there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God.' The manna-sabbath was a weekly enacted prophecy of the eschatological rest that Christ's people enter at the consummation.

33.note: Moses commands an omer of manna to be placed 'before the LORD' for perpetual keeping. Hebrews 9:4 lists this golden urn among the Mosaic tabernacle contents — a permanent witness to the wilderness provision kept in the Most Holy Place. As the manna was preserved before God, Christ the true bread is perpetually 'before the Father' as our high priest (Heb 9:24).

5.note: Water from the rock — that rock was Christ

6.note: Paul identifies the rock struck at Horeb with Christ: 'they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and the Rock was Christ.' The structure is exact — the leader's rod strikes the rock, judgment-water flows, the people live. The cross is the antitype: Christ is struck so that the living water of the Spirit flows (John 7:37-39; 19:34).

On the last day of the Feast of Tabernacles, Jesus cries: 'If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink.' The feast included a water-pouring ceremony recalling the Meribah miracle; Jesus' invitation transposes the OT rock-water sign into his own person. The one who stood before the rock is the Rock (1 Cor 10:4).

Behold I will stand before you on the rock at Horeb and you shall strike the rock and water shall come out — Paul identifies this rock explicitly with Christ: the rock was Christ; the water-giving rock in the wilderness is the type of the one who gives living water (John 7:38-39)

Rivers of living water will flow from within him — Jesus's promise at the Feast of Tabernacles echoes the water-from-the-rock tradition; the rock that gave water is the type, Christ is the antitype who gives the Spirit as living water

11.note: Moses' arms raised in prayer while Joshua fights below — when they fall, Israel loses; when they are held up, Israel prevails. This is an early figuration of intercessory mediation. Hebrews 7:25 says Christ 'always lives to make intercession' for those who draw near to God through him — the perfect mediator whose arms never need supporting.

Moses interceding on the hill while the battle rages in the valley anticipates Paul's confidence: 'Christ Jesus is the one who died — more than that, who was raised — who is at the right hand of God, who indeed is interceding for us.' Aaron and Hur support Moses' physical arms; the resurrection supports Christ's perpetual intercession.

14.note: God commands Moses to write the defeat of Amalek in a book 'as a memorial' — the Book of Remembrance where names are recorded and enemies are blotted out. Revelation 3:5 promises: 'I will never blot his name out of the book of life.' The Amalek-record introduces the motif of the divine ledger where foes are erased and the faithful are retained.

11.note: Jethro's acknowledgment — 'now I know that the LORD is greater than all gods' — is a paradigmatic Gentile confession prompted by witnessing the exodus. Stephen's note that 'Moses was instructed in all the wisdom of the Egyptians' precedes the irony that a Midianite priest gave Moses wiser counsel than any Egyptian sage could. Gentile recognition of YHWH's supremacy points toward the universal mission.

21.note: Jethro tells Moses to select 'able men, such as fear God, men who are trustworthy and hate a bribe' to handle judicial cases. When the apostles are overwhelmed in Acts 6:1-4, they tell the community to 'pick out from among you seven men of good repute, full of the Spirit and of wisdom.' Luke's narrative is structured by the same problem and the same solution: the appointed leader delegates to qualified sub-leaders.

The qualifications Jethro specifies — able, God-fearing, trustworthy, honest — anticipate the deacon qualifications in 1 Timothy 3:8 ('dignified, not double-tongued, not addicted to much wine, not greedy for dishonest gain'). The Exodus pattern of distributed, character-qualified leadership becomes the template for church order.

23.note: Jethro's argument: Moses cannot carry the whole burden alone — 'you and the people with you will wear yourselves out.' The solution is to build up the people to handle their own cases. Ephesians 4:12 is the NT fulfillment of this logic: Christ gave gifted leaders 'to equip the saints for the work of ministry, for building up the body of Christ' — not to do all the ministry themselves.

5.note: You are a chosen people, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's special possession — Peter applies the Sinai covenant identity of Exod 19:5-6 directly to the church. The identity terms that YHWH declared over Israel at Sinai (treasured possession, kingdom of priests, holy nation) are now applied to the multi-ethnic community of Christ: the new covenant community receives the same covenant vocation that Israel received at the covenant's founding.

To him who loves us and has freed us from our sins by his blood, and has made us to be a kingdom and priests to serve his God and Father — Revelation applies the kingdom-of-priests language of Exod 19:6 to the redeemed. The Sinai covenant description (a kingdom of priests and a holy nation) was always intended as the pattern for a community that mediates the divine presence to the world; Revelation declares it fulfilled in the blood-redeemed community.

You shall be to me a kingdom of priests and a holy nation — the Sinai covenant charter (Exod 19:5-6) is applied to the church in 1 Pet 2:9: you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession; the church inherits the covenant community's calling

He has made us a kingdom, priests to his God and Father — Revelation applies Exod 19:6 to the redeemed: Christ has made us a kingdom and priests; the Sinai covenant's Israel-as-kingdom-of-priests is fulfilled in the church through the new covenant
A royal priesthood, a holy nation, God's special possession

18.note: You have not come to a mountain that can be touched and that is burning with fire; to darkness, gloom and storm; to a trumpet blast or to such a voice speaking words that those who heard it begged that no further word be spoken to them — Hebrews explicitly contrasts the terrifying Sinai theophany of Exod 19:16-19 with the new covenant approach to heavenly Zion. The smoke, fire, darkness, trumpet, and terrified Israelites who begged Moses to speak instead of God mark the limits of the old covenant mediation.

2.note: You shall not murder / commit adultery / steal — the Decalogue (Exod 20) is the background for the Sermon on the Mount's antitheses; Jesus does not abolish but fulfills, deepening the law to its heart-level intent

You shall not covet — Paul cites the tenth commandment as the law that showed him sin: I would not have known covetousness if the law had not said You shall not covet; the Decalogue is the very place where the law's diagnostic function is clearest

12.note: Honor your father and mother — which is the first commandment with a promise — so that it may go well with you and that you may enjoy long life on the earth — Paul quotes the Fifth Commandment of Exod 20:12 in his household code, identifying it as the first commandment accompanied by a specific promise (long life in the land). The Sinai commandment regulating the family structure is embedded in the new covenant community ethic.
Honor your father and mother — first commandment with promise

13.note: Commandments summed up in love for neighbor
Do not murder -- Jesus deepens this to anger and contempt

14.note: Do not commit adultery -- Jesus deepens this to lustful intent

17.note: I would not have known what sin was had it not been for the law. For I would not have known what it was to covet if the law had not said, You shall not covet — Paul's foundational statement about Torah's role in revealing sin centers on the tenth commandment of Exod 20:17. Of all the Decalogue's prohibitions, the covetousness command is the one Paul singles out as the point where the law disclosed his own hidden sinfulness, since covetousness (unlike the other commands) cannot be externally performed but only internally harbored.

24.note: You have heard that it was said, 'Eye for eye and tooth for tooth.' But I tell you, do not resist an evil person — Jesus cites the lex talionis of Exod 21:24 and radicalizes it. The Exodus context is judicial (proportional punishment by courts, forbidding excessive retaliation), not personal vengeance. Jesus moves from the judicial register to the personal and relational: the person who has absorbed a wrong is called not to seek judicial compensation but to practice active non-retaliation and enemy-love.
Eye for an eye -- Jesus calls to non-retaliation

32.note: Then what was spoken by Jeremiah the prophet was fulfilled: They took the thirty pieces of silver, the price set on him by the people of Israel, and they used them to buy the potter's field — the thirty pieces of silver that Judas received for betraying Jesus (Matt 26:15) echoes both Exod 21:32 (the compensation for a gored slave is thirty shekels of silver) and Zech 11:12-13. The slave-price of Exod 21:32 is the legal background for the price set on the life of the one who was handed over.

21.note: When a foreigner resides among you in your land, do not mistreat them. The foreigner residing among you must be treated as your native-born. Love them as yourself, for you were foreigners in Egypt — the Holiness Code reinforces and extends the Covenant Code's foreigner protection of Exod 22:21 with the same motivation (you were foreigners in Egypt). The legal protection of the vulnerable foreigner, grounded in Israel's own experience of oppression, becomes a canonical constant across the law codes.

25.note: If you lend to those from whom you expect repayment, what credit is that to you? Even sinners lend to sinners, expecting full repayment. But love your enemies, do good to them, and lend to them without expecting to get anything back — Jesus's instruction on lending without interest radicalizes the Covenant Code of Exod 22:25 (do not charge interest to the poor). The Exodus command protects the poor from debt-exploitation; Jesus extends the logic: the kingdom standard is lending without expectation of return, not merely without interest.

4.note: Love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you — Jesus's command to love enemies has an OT precedent in the Covenant Code: if you come across your enemy's ox or donkey wandering off, be sure to return it (Exod 23:4). The Covenant Code requires practical care for the enemy's property; Jesus radicalizes this from property-care to active love and prayer. The seeds of enemy-love ethics are already in the Exodus legal tradition.

20.note: They drank from the spiritual rock that accompanied them, and that rock was Christ — Paul's identification of Christ as the pre-existent companion of Israel in the wilderness applies to the same period when the angel of Exod 23:20 led Israel (I am sending an angel ahead of you). The angel whose name was YHWH (he bears my name, v.21) is the divine presence that accompanied Israel; Paul identifies this accompanying presence as Christ, the pre-incarnate Son.

8.note: This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins — Jesus's words at the Last Supper are a deliberate echo of Moses's words in Exod 24:8: this is the blood of the covenant. The Sinai covenant was ratified with the blood of animals sprinkled on the people; the new covenant is ratified with Christ's own blood. Jesus substitutes himself for the sacrificial animals, identifying himself as the covenant sacrifice whose blood seals the definitive covenant.

When Moses had proclaimed every command of the law to all the people, he took the blood of calves... and sprinkled the scroll and all the people. He said, This is the blood of the covenant that God has commanded you to keep — Hebrews explicitly quotes Exod 24:8 in its systematic comparison of the Mosaic and new covenants. The Sinai blood-sprinkling is the old covenant type whose anti-type is Christ's blood offered once for all in the heavenly sanctuary.

This is the blood of the covenant that God commanded for you — Hebrews cites Exod 24:8 in the context of Christ's blood as the new covenant's ratifying blood; the old covenant's blood-sprinkling is the type of the new covenant's once-for-all blood
Blood of the covenant — new covenant sealed by Christ's blood

11.note: Blessed are those who are invited to the wedding supper of the Lamb — the eschatological wedding banquet of Revelation has its OT prototype in the Sinai covenant meal of Exod 24:11: the elders of Israel saw God and ate and drank. The unusual Sinai vision-meal (they saw God and survived to eat in his presence) is the covenant-ratifying banquet that anticipates the eschatological banquet where the redeemed eat and drink in the presence of God.

16.note: After six days Jesus took Peter, James and John with him and led them up a high mountain by themselves. There he was transfigured before them... a bright cloud covered them — the Transfiguration deliberately replicates the Sinai pattern of Exod 24:16: after six days the glory settled on Sinai, and on the seventh day YHWH called from within the cloud. After six days, on a mountain, with a cloud and divine voice, Jesus is revealed in glory — the Transfiguration is a Christological recapitulation of the Sinai theophany, presenting Jesus as the one who fulfills and surpasses Moses.

8.note: The divine command to build a sanctuary 'that I may dwell in their midst' is the direct antecedent of John 1:14: 'the Word became flesh and dwelt [eskēnōsen — lit. tabernacled] among us.' John uses the tabernacle verb deliberately; the incarnation is God doing what Exodus 25:8 inaugurated, now in human flesh rather than acacia wood.

The tabernacle is built 'exactly as I show you' — according to the heavenly pattern (Heb. tabnît). Hebrews 8:5 cites this command to prove the Mosaic sanctuary is 'a copy and shadow of the heavenly things.' The earthly tabernacle is real, but reality itself is in heaven, and Jesus ministers in the true tent (Heb 8:2).

'I will dwell in their midst' — the tabernacle promise becomes the eschatological promise of Revelation 21:3: 'the dwelling place of God is with man. He will dwell with them, and they will be his people.' The trajectory from the wilderness sanctuary to the New Jerusalem runs through the incarnation.

9.note: Exactly as I show you concerning the pattern of the tabernacle — the Sinai tabernacle was built according to the heavenly pattern (tabnit); Hebrews argues that the earthly tabernacle is a shadow and copy of the heavenly sanctuary where Christ now ministers as high priest

17.note: Paul calls Christ the hilastērion (propitiation/mercy seat) 'by his blood' — the exact word (LXX kappōret, Heb. kappōret) used for the gold cover of the ark where the high priest sprinkled blood on Yom Kippur. Christ does not merely use the mercy seat; he IS the meeting place of judgment and mercy, the locus where God's wrath is propitiated and his covenant faithfulness displayed.

'I will meet with you from above the mercy seat' — this is the OT 'throne of grace.' The author of Hebrews takes up the image: 'let us draw near with confidence to the throne of grace, that we may receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need.' Access that was restricted to Aaron once a year is now open to every believer through Christ.

The mercy seat received blood to atone for Israel's sin year after year. John declares: 'He is the propitiation [hilasmos] for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world.' The kappōret's annual, national, repetitive atonement is replaced by the once-for-all, universal propitiation in Christ.

22.note: 'There I will meet with you, and from above the mercy seat...I will speak with you.' Hebrews 9:5 notes that above the ark were 'the cherubim of glory overshadowing the mercy seat' — the divine meeting-place that the author can only say, 'of these things we cannot now speak in detail,' because Christ has rendered the whole system both fulfilled and obsolete.

30.note: The Bread of the Presence (leḥem haPānîm — 'bread of the face/presence') must remain before the LORD continually. Jesus 'I am the bread of life' (John 6:35) claims to be the permanent presence-bread: not the weekly-changed loaves that nourished priests, but the living bread that comes down from heaven so that whoever eats of him will live forever (6:51).

31.note: The seven-branched menorah that gives light to the sanctuary anticipates Jesus' 'I am the light of the world' (John 8:12). The menorah burned olive oil that Moses was to keep burning perpetually (27:20); Christ is himself the undying light — not oil-dependent but self-luminous — whom Revelation 21:23 calls 'the lamp' of the New Jerusalem.

John sees seven golden lampstands in his inaugural vision (Rev 1:12-20), which Christ identifies as the seven churches. The menorah pattern governs the image: the local church is the tabernacle lamp in its community, the lampstand that holds Christ's light before the world. The menorah's form is 'borrowed' to describe the church's vocation.

40.note: The ark of the covenant with the mercy seat — Hebrews describes the tabernacle furnishings (golden lampstand, table, bread of the Presence, incense altar, ark with mercy seat) to show that the old covenant's physical sanctuary points to the greater heavenly sanctuary accessed through Christ's blood
Pattern shown on the mountain — tabernacle a shadow of the heavenly

31.note: The veil separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place (v.33 — 'the veil shall separate for you the Holy Place from the Most Holy') is the OT type of Christ's flesh: Hebrews 10:20 calls his body the 'new and living way' opened through 'the curtain, that is, through his flesh.' The veil was torn at his death (Matt 27:51) — his flesh broken so the way into God's presence stands open.

The veil woven with blue, purple, and crimson threads at Exodus 26:31 is the curtain that tears 'from top to bottom' at Jesus' death (Matt 27:51; Mark 15:38). The tearing from top (God's side) to bottom signifies that it is God who opens the way — not human effort. What the tabernacle kept closed, the crucifixion opens permanently.

The inner sanctuary behind the veil was the most restricted space in Israel's worship — only the high priest, once a year. Hebrews 6:19 says Christian hope 'enters into the inner place behind the curtain, where Jesus has gone as a forerunner on our behalf.' The veil no longer bars; Christ has entered for us and keeps the way open.

33.note: The veil's function — dividing holy from most holy — is the Spirit's object lesson: 'the Holy Spirit indicates that the way into the holy places is not yet opened as long as the first section is still standing' (Heb 9:8). The tabernacle's architecture was itself a prophecy of inadequacy, pointing to the day when the barrier would fall.

1.note: The bronze altar of burnt offering — the first thing approached when entering the tabernacle court — is where every sacrifice was consumed. Hebrews 13:10 declares: 'We have an altar from which those who serve the tent have no right to eat.' The Christian's altar is the cross; the sacrifice is Christ himself. The bronze altar's centrality in worship prefigures the cross's centrality in the new covenant.

The bronze altar's blood-sacrifices — bulls and goats — are the explicit point of contrast in Hebrews 9:13: 'if the blood of goats and bulls...sanctify for the purification of the flesh, how much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God, purify our conscience from dead works.' The altar's sacrifices make sense only as the shadow of Calvary.

20.note: 'You shall command the people of Israel to bring you pure beaten olive oil for the light, that a lamp may regularly be set up to burn.' The perpetual oil-light of the tabernacle is the backdrop for Jesus' 'I am the light of the world' — spoken in the treasury, near the lampstand hall during the Feast of Tabernacles, a festival with torch-lighting ceremonies that evoked the wilderness cloud and fire.

The tabernacle lamp must burn always — the perpetual light representing the divine presence. Revelation 21:23 declares of the New Jerusalem: 'the city has no need of sun or moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light, and its lamp is the Lamb.' The oil-fed menorah gives way to the self-luminous Lamb; the shadow of perpetual light becomes the substance of uncreated light.

29.note: He is able to save completely those who come to God through him, because he always lives to intercede for them — Aaron bearing the names of all twelve tribes over his heart on the breastpiece of decision as a continual remembrance before the LORD is the OT priestly type of Christ's perpetual intercession. Where Aaron physically carried the names into the sanctuary, Christ bears his people before the Father in his ongoing high-priestly ministry at the right hand of God.

41.note: You have an anointing from the Holy One, and all of you know the truth — the anointing that sets apart the Aaronic priests for service in Exod 28:41 has its NT counterpart in the Spirit's anointing of the whole community as a royal priesthood (1 Pet 2:9). Where Exod 28 anoints specific men for a mediating office, the new covenant anointing is democratized: all the people of God are anointed, just as Moses prophesied the Spirit would be poured on all (Num 11:29).

18.note: Christ loved us and gave himself up for us as a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God — Paul's description of Christ's atoning death uses the exact Levitical formula of the ordination-sacrifice in Exod 29:18: a food offering, an aroma pleasing to the LORD. The ordination sacrifice that consecrates the priests through a fragrant burnt offering is the type for Christ's self-offering at the cross, which consecrates the new priestly community through the ultimate pleasing-aroma sacrifice.

38.note: Day after day every priest stands and performs his religious duties; again and again he offers the same sacrifices, which can never take away sins. But when this priest had offered for all time one sacrifice for sins, he sat down at the right hand of God — the perpetual tamid (daily) offering of Exod 29:38-42 (two lambs a year old, one in the morning and one at twilight, every day) is the specific contrast Hebrews constructs against Christ's once-for-all sacrifice. The repetition of the daily lambs demonstrates their insufficiency; Christ's singular offering demonstrates its finality.

45.note: The Word became flesh and made his dwelling among us. We have seen his glory, the glory of the one and only Son, who came from the Father, full of grace and truth — John's incarnation statement (eskenen = literally tabernacled) is the fulfillment of the purpose YHWH stated in Exod 29:45: I will dwell among the Israelites and be their God. The entire tabernacle-construction project was directed toward this goal (so that I might dwell among them), which is realized not in a tent but in the incarnation of the divine Son.

Now the dwelling of God is with men, and he will live with them. They will be his people, and God himself will be with them and be their God — Revelation's eschatological climax declares the ultimate fulfillment of Exod 29:45's covenant-dwelling goal: YHWH who desired to dwell with Israel in a tabernacle now dwells permanently with his redeemed from every nation. The tabernacle-purpose initiated in Exodus reaches its eschatological realization in the new Jerusalem.

1.note: The altar of incense before the veil — where fragrant smoke rose before the LORD daily — is the OT type of the heavenly intercession depicted in Revelation 8:3-4: 'another angel came and stood at the altar with a golden censer, and he was given much incense to offer with the prayers of all the saints on the golden altar before the throne.' The incense-prayer equation was already implicit in the earthly tabernacle.

The incense altar is the setting for Zechariah's encounter with Gabriel: 'the whole multitude of the people were praying outside at the hour of incense' (Luke 1:10). The incense hour was a liturgical moment of concentrated prayer throughout Israel — demonstrating that the altar's symbolism was alive in Second Temple practice, just before the incarnation.

12.note: The half-shekel ransom per person ('you shall give it to the LORD as atonement for your lives') establishes the principle that life requires a ransom. Peter explicitly contrasts this: 'you were ransomed from the futile ways inherited from your forefathers, not with perishable things such as silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ.' The atonement money points to its own inadequacy.

18.note: The bronze basin where Aaron and his sons must wash before entering the tent — unwashed, they die (v.20) — is the OT washing of priestly preparation. Ephesians 5:26 applies the same language to the church: Christ 'cleansed her by the washing of water with the word.' The basin's purification-for-service becomes the regenerative washing of the new covenant.

Jesus washing the disciples' feet at the Last Supper enacts the basin-rite for the new covenant priesthood: 'the one who has bathed does not need to wash, except for his feet' (John 13:10). The tabernacle basin required washing before service; Jesus' washing commissions the disciples for service in his kingdom.

23.note: The sacred anointing oil — the mark of priests, tabernacle furnishings, and later kings — is poured on Aaron and his sons to consecrate them (29:7; 30:30). Peter declares the antitype: 'God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and with power.' The title 'Christ' (Heb. Māšîaḥ) means the Anointed One; every drop of the tabernacle oil pointed to this singular anointing.

The anointing oil consecrated priests uniquely — it could not be replicated or put on an outsider (v.32-33). John extends anointing to all believers: 'you have been anointed by the Holy One, and you all have knowledge.' The Spirit's anointing of the whole church is the fulfillment of Moses' longing in Numbers 11:29 ('Would that all the LORD's people were prophets!').

34.note: The sacred incense — made of rare spices, holy and reserved solely for the LORD — is the backdrop for Paul's metaphor: 'we are the aroma of Christ to God among those who are being saved and among those who are perishing.' The incense that could only ascend before God now ascends in the lives of those who carry the fragrance of the gospel into the world.

Paul calls the Philippians' gift 'a fragrant offering, a sacrifice acceptable and pleasing to God' — language drawn from the incense and burnt-offering ceremonies. The priestly offering of incense is reinterpreted as the community's acts of generosity and love that ascend to God as a pleasing aroma.

3.note: Bezalel is 'filled with the Spirit of God, with ability and intelligence, with knowledge and all craftsmanship' for building the tabernacle. This is the earliest explicit Spirit-gifting for specific service in Scripture. Paul's theology of spiritual gifts (1 Cor 12:7-11) mirrors the pattern exactly: the Spirit distributes gifts 'to each one individually as he wills' for building up the community — the new tabernacle is the body of Christ.

13.note: The Sabbath is declared 'a sign between me and you throughout your generations' — a perpetual covenant marker embedded in creation rhythm. Paul identifies the Sabbath as part of the 'shadow of things to come' (Col 2:17), while 'the substance belongs to Christ.' The weekly rest was always pointing beyond itself to the consummation-rest that Christ's work inaugurates.

The Sabbath command reissued here to the covenant people during tabernacle construction emphasizes rest as a divine pattern, not merely a human need. Hebrews 4:9-10 concludes: 'there remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God, for whoever has entered God's rest has also rested from his works as God did from his.' The sign points to an ultimate entering-in.

4.note: Stephen cites the golden calf directly: 'they made a calf in those days, and offered a sacrifice to the idol, and were rejoicing in the works of their hands.' For Stephen, the calf is the first link in the chain of Israel's rejection of God-appointed leaders (Moses, the prophets, Jesus) — idol-worship as the recurring symptom of the same disease.

6.note: Paul quotes the LXX of this verse exactly: 'the people sat down to eat and drink and rose up to play.' He deploys it as a direct warning: 'do not be idolaters, as some of them were' (1 Cor 10:7). The golden calf feast becomes the NT paradigm for the danger of idolatry in the wilderness of Christian life, specifically in relation to the Lord's Supper and feasts offered to idols.

The people sat down to eat and drink and rose up to play — Paul cites Exod 32:6 (the golden calf incident) as a warning against idolatry: do not be idolaters as some of them were; the wilderness generation's failure is the warning example for the Corinthians tempted by idol feasts
The people sat to eat and drink and rose to play

11.note: Moses 'implored' the LORD in the presence of God, interceding on the basis of covenant promise: 'Remember Abraham, Isaac, and Israel, your servants, to whom you swore by your own self...' This mediatorial intercession is the OT type of Christ who 'always lives to make intercession' for those who draw near to God through him. Moses' intercession was effective; Christ's is perfect and permanent.

Moses' argument — 'why should the Egyptians say that you brought them out to kill them?' — grounds God's faithfulness in his own reputation. Paul's analogous argument in Romans 11: 'Has God rejected his people? By no means!' grounds the preservation of Israel in God's election. Both appeals to covenant faithfulness, both answered by divine mercy rather than judgment.

30.note: Moses tells the people: 'You have sinned a great sin. And now I will go up to the LORD; perhaps I can make atonement for your sin.' Moses ascends the mountain as a mediating priest attempting to bear Israel's sin — but his atonement is uncertain ('perhaps'). Hebrews 9:28 declares the certain antitype: 'Christ, having been offered once to bear the sins of many, will appear a second time, not to deal with sin but to save those who are eagerly waiting for him.'

32.note: Moses offers to be 'blotted out of your book' if Israel cannot be forgiven — a substitutionary self-sacrifice that the LORD refuses. Paul echoes the same sacrificial love: 'I could wish that I myself were accursed and cut off from Christ for the sake of my brothers.' But neither Moses nor Paul could accomplish this; only Christ actually bore the curse (Gal 3:13). The pattern of love-that-would-substitute finds its only true fulfillment in the cross.

7.note: Moses took the tent and pitched it outside the camp — the tent of meeting outside the camp prefigures Jesus suffering outside the gate (Heb 13:12-13); the sacred meeting-space was outside the camp, as the place of sacrifice was outside Jerusalem

11.note: I no longer call you servants, because a servant does not know his master's business. Instead, I have called you friends, for I have told you everything I have learned from my Father — Jesus's gift of friendship to the disciples extends to all believers the intimate face-to-face speech that the LORD had with Moses in Exod 33:11. The unique prophetic intimacy of Moses (YHWH spoke to him face to face, as one speaks to a friend) is democratized in the new covenant: all who follow Jesus are brought into the same intimacy.

14.note: Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest... for I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls — Jesus's rest-offer to the weary echoes YHWH's rest-promise to Moses in Exod 33:14 (my Presence will go with you, and I will give you rest). What YHWH promised as the consequence of his accompanying presence, Jesus now embodies: he himself is the Presence whose companionship produces the rest Moses sought for Israel.

19.note: I will have mercy on whom I have mercy

20.note: No one has ever seen God, but the one and only Son, who is himself God and is in closest relationship with the Father, has made him known — John 1:18's foundational theological claim that no one has seen God presupposes the Sinai limit of Exod 33:20 (no one may see me and live). What Moses was denied (direct vision of YHWH's face) and only partially received (YHWH's back) is made accessible in the incarnate Son: the one who has seen me has seen the Father (John 14:9).

6.note: The Word became flesh and made his dwelling among us. We have seen his glory, the glory of the one and only Son, who came from the Father, full of grace and truth — John's description of Christ as full of grace and truth (charis kai aletheia) is the Greek rendering of the hesed we-emet (covenant love and faithfulness) that YHWH proclaimed as his name in Exod 34:6. The divine self-disclosure on Sinai (compassionate, gracious, abounding in love and faithfulness) is what Moses saw and what John declares is now fully visible in Christ.

29.note: We are not like Moses, who would put a veil over his face to keep the Israelites from gazing at it while the radiance was fading away... but whenever anyone turns to the Lord, the veil is taken away... and we all, who with unveiled faces contemplate the Lord's glory, are being transformed into his image with ever-increasing glory — Paul's extended treatment of the Moses veil interprets Exod 34:29-35 Christologically. Moses veiled a glory that was passing away; in Christ, the glory does not fade but transforms the beholder. The veil that hid Moses's face is removed in Christ, and unveiled contemplation becomes the mechanism of transformation.

Moses did not know that the skin of his face shone because he had been talking with God — the veil Moses put over his face (Exod 34:33-35) is Paul's central image in 2 Cor 3: the old covenant glory was fading and veiled; the new covenant's greater glory is unveiled; the veil is removed in Christ

His face shone like the sun — the Transfiguration's shining face of Jesus echoes Moses's shining face at Sinai; Jesus is the new and greater Moses whose glory is not derivative but intrinsic, not fading but permanent
Moses' veil over his face — veil removed in Christ

31.note: There are different kinds of gifts, but the same Spirit... to each one the manifestation of the Spirit is given for the common good — the Spirit-filling of Bezalel with wisdom, understanding, knowledge and skill for building the tabernacle (Exod 35:31-35) is the OT precedent for the NT theology of spiritual gifts. Both texts describe the Spirit distributing specific capacities to specific individuals for the construction of God's sacred dwelling: the Mosaic tabernacle then, the church as the Spirit's temple now.
